title: Ocean: An Ode
content:
혻혻혻혻 occasioned by His Majesty's Royal Encouragement of the Sea Service.


혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻 To Which is Prefixed an Ode to the King; and a Discourse on Ode.


혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻I think myself obliged to recommend to you a consideration of the greatest importance; and I should look upon it as a great happiness, if, at the beginning of my reign, I could see the foundation laid of so great and necessary a work, as the increase and encouragement of our seamen in general; that they may be invited, rather than compelled by force and violence, to enter into the service of their country, as oft as occasion shall require it: a consideration worthy the representatives of a people great and flourishing in trade and navigation. This leads me to mention to you the case of Greenwich Hospital, that care may be taken, by some addition to that fund, to render comfortable and effectual that charitable provision, for the support and maintenance of our seamen, worn out, and become decrepit by age and infirmities, in the service of their country.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻[Speech, Jan. 27, 1727-8.]


혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻To the King.--1728.


혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Old ocean's praise
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Demands my lays;
혻혻혻혻A truly British theme I sing;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A theme so great,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻I dare complete,
혻혻혻혻And join with ocean, ocean's king.

혻혻혻혻The Roman ode
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Majestic flow'd:
혻혻혻혻Its stream divinely clear, and strong;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻In sense, and sound,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Thebes roll'd profound;
혻혻혻혻The torrent roar'd and foam'd along.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Let Thebes, nor Rome,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻So fam'd, presume
혻혻혻혻To triumph o'er a northern isle;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Late time shall know
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The north can glow,
혻혻혻혻If dread Augustus deign to smile.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The naval crown
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Is all his own!
혻혻혻혻Our fleet, if war, or commerce, call,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻His will performs
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Through waves and storms,
혻혻혻혻And rides in triumph round the ball.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻No former race,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻With strong embrace,
혻혻혻혻This theme to ravish durst aspire;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻With virgin charms
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻My soul it warms,
혻혻혻혻And melts melodious on my lyre.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻My lays I file
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻With cautious toil;
혻혻혻혻Ye graces! turn the glowing lines;
혻혻혻혻On anvils neat
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Your strokes repeat;
혻혻혻혻At every stroke the work refines!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻How music charms!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻How metre warms!
혻혻혻혻Parent of actions, good and brave!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻How vice it tames!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And worth inflames!
혻혻혻혻And holds proud empire o'er the grave!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Jove mark'd for man
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A scanty span,
혻혻혻혻But lent him wings to fly his doom;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Wit scorns the grave;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻To wit he gave
혻혻혻혻The life of gods! immortal bloom!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Since years will fly,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And pleasures die,
혻혻혻혻Day after day, as years advance;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Since, while life lasts,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Joy suffers blasts
혻혻혻혻From frowning fate, and fickle chance;

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Nor life is long;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But soon we throng,
혻혻혻혻Like autumn leaves, death's pallid shore;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻We make, at least,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Of bad the best,
혻혻혻혻If in life's phantom, fame, we soar.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Our strains divide
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The laurel's pride;
혻혻혻혻With those we lift to life, to live;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻By fame enroll'd
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻With heroes bold,
혻혻혻혻And share the blessings which we give.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻What hero's praise
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Can fire my lays,
혻혻혻혻Like his, with whom my lay begun?
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Justice sincere,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And courage clear,
혻혻혻혻Rise the two columns of his throne.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"How form'd for sway!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Who look, obey;
혻혻혻혻They read the monarch in his port:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Their love and awe
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Supply the law;
혻혻혻혻And his own lustre makes the court:"

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻On yonder height,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻What golden light
혻혻혻혻Triumphant shines? and shines alone?
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Unrivall'd blaze!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The nations gaze!
혻혻혻혻'Tis not the sun; 'tis Britain's throne.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Our monarch, there,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Rear'd high in air,
혻혻혻혻Should tempests rise, disdains to bend;
혻혻혻혻Like British oak,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Derides the stroke;
혻혻혻혻His blooming honours far extend!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Beneath them lies,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻With lifted eyes,
혻혻혻혻Fair Albion, like an amorous maid;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻While interest wings
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Bold foreign kings
혻혻혻혻To fly, like eagles, to his shade.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻At his proud foot
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The sea, pour'd out,
혻혻혻혻Immortal nourishment supplies;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Thence wealth and state,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And power and fate,
혻혻혻혻Which Europe reads in George's eyes.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻From what we view,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻We take the clue,
혻혻혻혻Which leads from great to greater thing
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Men doubt no more,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But gods adore,
혻혻혻혻When such resemblance shines in kings.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Edward Young represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Edward Young's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
